prompt:
Implement an agent notebook system for persistent knowledge across conversations. See issue #19 for the full design.

## Context

reel-life is a media curation agent (Go, Claude API via anthropic-sdk-go). The agent manages Sonarr/Radarr/Prowlarr/Overseerr via tool-use loops. The codebase follows a consistent pattern:
- Tools are defined in `internal/agent/tools.go` as `toolDef` structs with `anthropic.ToolParam` + `Destructive bool`
- Tool input types are structs with jsonschema tags in the same file
- Tool dispatch is a big switch in `internal/agent/agent.go:dispatchTool()`
- Tool defs are grouped by integration (e.g., `sonarrToolDefs()`, `overseerrToolDefs()`) and combined in `allToolDefs()`
- Config lives in `internal/config/config.go` with YAML + env var overrides
- Optional integrations are nil-checked in dispatch (e.g., `if a.overseerr == nil { return error }`)
- The system prompt is a const `systemPrompt` in `internal/agent/agent.go`
- Wiring happens in `cmd/reel-life/main.go`

## What to build

### 1. Storage layer: `internal/notebook/notebook.go`

Create a new `internal/notebook` package. Use a single JSON file for storage (path configurable via config).

Each note has:
- `id` (string, UUID or short random ID)
- `type` — `pinned` or `reference`
- `title` (string)
- `content` (string)
- `created_at`, `updated_at` (time.Time)

Provide a `Notebook` interface and a `FileNotebook` implementation with methods:
- `Write(ctx, note) error` — create or update (upsert by ID)
- `Read(ctx, id) (Note, error)` — get by ID
- `Search(ctx, query) ([]Note, error)` — simple substring match on title+content
- `List(ctx) ([]NoteSummary, error)` — returns id, type, title, updated_at (not full content)
- `Delete(ctx, id) error`
- `Pinned(ctx) ([]Note, error)` — returns all pinned notes (used for prompt injection)

Use file locking or atomic writes to avoid corruption. Keep it simple — no database.

Enforce limits:
- Max 10 pinned notes
- Max pinned content total ~2000 chars (to keep prompt size manageable)
- Reference notes can be more numerous (cap at maybe 100)

### 2. Agent tools: add to `internal/agent/tools.go`

Add a `notebookToolDefs()` function following the existing pattern. Four tools:

- `notebook_write` (destructive) — input: `type` (pinned/reference), `title`, `content`, optional `id` (for updates)
- `notebook_read` — input: `id`
- `notebook_list` — no required input, optional `type` filter
- `notebook_delete` (destructive) — input: `id`

Add `notebook_write` and `notebook_delete` to the `destructiveTools` map.

Wire into `allToolDefs()` like the other integrations.

### 3. Tool dispatch: add cases to `internal/agent/agent.go`

- Add a `notebook` field to the `Agent` struct (type `notebook.Notebook`, can be nil)
- Update `New()` and `NewWithClient()` to accept the notebook
- Add dispatch cases for the four notebook tools, nil-checking like other optional integrations
- The `notebook_write` tool should call `Search` first with the title to avoid creating duplicate notes with similar titles — return the existing note's ID in the result so the agent can update instead

### 4. System prompt integration

- In `agent.go:Process()`, before building the message, call `notebook.Pinned()` and append the pinned notes to the system prompt as a clearly delimited section, e.g.:

```
## Notebook (always loaded)
### [title]
[content]
...
```

- Also add guidance to the system prompt telling the agent:
  - It has a notebook for persistent memory across conversations
  - It should save useful observations: user preferences, recurring issues, operational patterns
  - Pinned notes are always visible; reference notes need to be looked up
  - Keep pinned notes concise and high-signal; use reference for detailed info
  - Check the notebook at the start of conversations about users it may have talked to before

### 5. Config

Add to `internal/config/config.go`:
- `NotebookConfig` struct with `Path string` (yaml: `path`) and `Enabled bool` (yaml: `enabled`)
- Add `Notebook NotebookConfig` to the main `Config` struct
- Add `NOTEBOOK_PATH` env var override
- Default path: `notebook.json` (relative to working dir)

### 6. Wiring in main.go

- Import the notebook package
- If notebook is enabled (or path is set), create a `FileNotebook` and pass to the agent
- Follow the same optional pattern as Radarr/Prowlarr/Overseerr

### 7. Tests

Write tests in `internal/notebook/notebook_test.go`:
- Test CRUD operations (write, read, list, delete)
- Test pinned note limits (max 10, max total content size)
- Test search functionality
- Test upsert behavior (write with existing ID updates)
- Test concurrent access if applicable
- Use temp directories for the JSON file

Also add agent-level tests in `internal/agent/agent_test.go` for the notebook tool dispatch (follow the existing test patterns in that file).

### Acceptance criteria
- `go build ./...` passes
- `go test ./...` passes
- `go vet ./...` passes  
- Pinned notes appear in the system prompt
- Notebook tools work in the dispatch loop
- Storage survives process restart (file-based)
- Limits are enforced (pinned count + size caps)
- No notebook config required (gracefully disabled when not configured)
